coredata.dc:title: Dual-modes of ratiometric fluorescent and smartphone-integrated colorimetric detection of glyphosate by carbon dots encapsulated porphyrin metal–organic frameworks
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Xueli
Huang, Gengli
Li, Yuchen
Guo, Jie
Chen, Xiaokai
Tan, Youwen
Tang, Wenzhi
Li, Zhonghong
coredata.dc:description: Ultrasensitive, reliable and prompt pre-warning of pesticide residue is critically significant to ensure environmental and food safety. Herein, a novel nanosensor fabricated by in situ encapsulating N-doped carbon dots into a porphyrin metal–organic framework was developed to ratiometric fluorescence (RF) and smartphone-integrated portable platform colorimetric dual-mode detection of glyphosate (GLP). The sensor responded to GLP by RF in the range of 0.01–6.67 mg/L with low limit of detection of 9.06 µg/L. Besides, a noticeable color evolution from pink purple to green under nature light was observed upon addition of GLP, thus a portable smartphone-integrated colorimetric platform was fabricated for visual colorimetric detecting GLP by incorporating the sensor with a smartphone and a 3D-printed mini-device. Interestingly, the platform can detect GLP merely by virtue of a built-in flashlight of the smartphone and Color Analyser Application. Additionally, desirable recoveries of 94.0–112.5 % and 90.0–113.6 % in spiked real samples were respectively obtained for RF and colorimetric channel. Furthermore, a one-to-one colorimetric logic gate (RGB vs GLP concentrations) offered the possibility of intelligent monitoring of GLP. Summarily, the versatile sensor satisfied laboratory instrumental analysis and was further integrated as a smartphone-assisted platform to furnish a novel tactic for quick GLP monitoring, particularly for resource-constrained settings.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: All agents were analytical reagent grade and used as obtained without further purification. Citric acid, tris(hydroxymethyl)methyl aminomethane (Tris), zirconium(IV) chloride (ZrCl 4 ), benzoic acid, tetrakis(4-carboxyphenyl)porphyrin) (TCPP), Amberlite®IRA402, glyphosate (GLP) as well as others pesticides, for instance, thiophanate-methyl (TM), 2, 4-dichloro phenoxy acetic acid (2,4-D), carbendazim (MBC), imidacloprid (IMI), temephos, trichlorfon, dimethoate, phosmet, paraoxon, dichlorvos (DDVP), carbaryl and chlorbenzuron (Chl) were provided by Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) and Primary-secondary amine (PSA) packing were respectively supplied by Chengdu Kelon Chemical Reagent Factory (Chengdu, Sichuan, China) and ANPEL Laboratory Technologies (Shanghai) Inc (Shanghai, China). Polyethylene glycol (PEG 6000), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), magnesium chloride hexahydrate (MgCl 2 ·6H 2 O), calcium chloride (CaCl 2 ), zinc chloride (ZnCl 2 ) and iron trichloride hexahydrate (FeCl 3 ·6H 2 O) were provided by Xilong Chemical Industry Co. ltd. (Shantou, Guangdong, China). Saccharides, such as sucrose (Suc) and glucose (Glu) and amino acids such as phenylalanine (Phe), cysteine (Cys), glycine (Gly), and tryptophan (Try) were obtained from Guangdong Chemical Regent Engineering-technological Research and Development Center (Shantou, Guangdong, China). Deionized water was used throughout the experimental procedures. Polyethersulfone (PES) membrane was supported by Jiangsu Green Union Science Instrument Co., ltd. (Taizhou, Jiangsu, China). The food samples were purchased from a local Hao & Duo supermarket (Yangling, Shaanxi, PR China). Tap water (pH = 7.56) and two kinds of river water samples were respectively sampled from a laboratory faucet, lacquer river (pH = 6.93) and Wei river (pH = 7.94).

head.ce:title: Dual-modes of ratiometric fluorescent and smartphone-integrated colorimetric detection of glyphosate by carbon dots encapsulated porphyrin metal–organic frameworks

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ultrasensitive, reliable and prompt pre-warning of pesticide residue is critically significant to ensure environmental and food safety. Herein, a novel nanosensor fabricated by in situ encapsulating N-doped carbon dots into a porphyrin metal–organic framework was developed to ratiometric fluorescence (RF) and smartphone-integrated portable platform colorimetric dual-mode detection of glyphosate (GLP). The sensor responded to GLP by RF in the range of 0.01–6.67 mg/L with low limit of detection of 9.06 µg/L. Besides, a noticeable color evolution from pink purple to green under nature light was observed upon addition of GLP, thus a portable smartphone-integrated colorimetric platform was fabricated for visual colorimetric detecting GLP by incorporating the sensor with a smartphone and a 3D-printed mini-device. Interestingly, the platform can detect GLP merely by virtue of a built-in flashlight of the smartphone and Color Analyser Application. Additionally, desirable recoveries of 94.0–112.5 % and 90.0–113.6 % in spiked real samples were respectively obtained for RF and colorimetric channel. Furthermore, a one-to-one colorimetric logic gate (RGB vs GLP concentrations) offered the possibility of intelligent monitoring of GLP. Summarily, the versatile sensor satisfied laboratory instrumental analysis and was further integrated as a smartphone-assisted platform to furnish a novel tactic for quick GLP monitoring, particularly for resource-constrained settings.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As an organophosphorus herbicide, glyphosate (GLP) inhibits the action of 5-enolpyruvylshikimate-3-phosphate synthase to prevent organisms from synthesizing three important amino acids (phenylalanine, tyrosine, and tryptophan) [1]. Accordingly, GLP is extensively applied as a non-selective herbicide to control weeds in farmland particularly for GLP-tolerant transgenic crops [2]. However, excessive spraying and improper handling have resulted in an increase of GLP residue in the environment and food matrices, which would eventually enter the human body via the food chain [3,4] and could potentially cause many chronic diseases [5,6]. Hence, to ensure environmental safety and food hygiene, different instrument assays [7], enzyme sensors [8] and favored chromatography [9,10] with high sensitivity and accuracy have been employed for detecting GLP. Nevertheless, these methods are high-cost, time-consuming and professional demanding, which impedes their practical application in routine assays and has prompted initiatives to exploit cost-effective, easy operation, rapid response methods for monitoring GLP.

Recent advances in versatile nanomaterials and novel techniques have opened up exciting possibilities for exploring diverse nanosensors. Optical sensors generating fluorescent (FL) signals hold exceptional promise in this regard due to their prompt sample-to-answer, simple manipulation and robust signal output [11,12]. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are emerging hybrid porous materials fabricated by self-assembling metal nodes and organic bridging ligands [11]. Among them, luminescent MOFs (LMOFs) were ideal candidates for FL sensors due to their tailorable structure, large surface area, functional-group-assisted target recognition and purposeful implantation of binding sites [13–15]. In particular, LMOFs sensors possessed conspicuously improved sensitivity owing to strong pre-concentration effect and signal amplification by promoting exciton migration via 3D networks [13,16,17]. To date, several LMOFs-based FL sensors have been fabricated for detecting GLP [15,18,19]. Nevertheless, most of them were single-emission, making them susceptible to analyte-independent factors (e.g. instrument settings and background light scattering) [20]. Ratiometric fluorescent (RF) sensors with two or more well-distinguished emissions provide an internal self-calibration for these factors, thus rendering higher sensitivity [2,21]. To simultaneously inherit the marvelous merits of LMOFs and obtain dual-emitting nanosensors, incorporating guest FL materials via physically hybriding [22] and post-synthetic modification [23] has been conducted to fabricate LMOFs-based RF sensors. These methods were usually involved in time-consuming and complicated operations. Hence, dual-emission LMOFs are desirable sensors only when they can be prepared by straightforward approaches, propelling researchers to propose a facile strategy for constructing LMOFs-based RF sensors.

Besides FL sensing, colorimetric-based optical detection has also elicited attention due to its low cost, easy handling, visual results and accessibility of implantation in portable devices for instrument-free analysis in the field or in resource-constrained regions [24,25]. However, most colorimetric sensors were single-signal detection, which may be impacted by extrinsic interferences and thus possibly provide uncertainty results in a complex matrix [26]. More undesirably, single-mode colorimetric may be failure to respond targets in some scenarios such as such as darked colored food samples [27,28]. In comparison, multi-mode sensing is more flexible to different actual scenarios because an applicable detection mode can be chosen according to the test conditions, available instruments and analysis demands for sample analysis [28]. Notably, multimodal sensors can integrate the unique merits of each sensing signal, considerably advancing the reliability of analysis via reciprocal verification of different readouts exhibiting independent signal transduction [29]. Therefore, developing a dual-mode detection integrating the high sensitivity of FL and the simplicity of the visual explanation in the colorimetric mode is urgently needed for GLP sensing. Further, due to faint perception of subtle changes, naked-eye colorimetric detection may only provide an approximate analyte level with inaccurate results [30,31]. Lately, smartphones have been served as emerging colorimetric readers by virtue of Red, Green and Blue (RGB) channel values, which can be retrieved by an inner camera photographing and an easy-to-access Color Recognition Application [14,32]. However, visual detection exclusively based on smartphone photographing was affected by environmental factors (e.g., external light intensity and distance between the sample and the phone camera), consequently decreasing the accuracy and reproducibility of results [33]. 3D printing emerges as an effective tool to solve the encountered problems by integrating smartphones and sensors with relevant 3D-printed accessories for constructing portable platforms [34].

Herein, a RF and smartphone-integrated portable platform colorimetric dual-mode detection of GLP was established using a nanosensor synthesized by in situ encapsulating N-doped CDs (N-CDs) into a zirconium (Zr) porphyrin metal–organic framework (PCN-222) (Scheme 1 ). The PCN-222 containing a porphyrin ligand tetrakis(4-carboxyphenyl)porphyrin) (TCPP), was chosen as the appealing FL and colorimetric dual signal generator due to its unique fluorescent and chromogenic motifs of tetrapyrrolic macrocycles. The PCN-222 played multiple roles in the sensor as: (1) a container of N-CDs for avoiding aggregation of N-CDs; (2) a pre-concentrator to enrich GLP to improve sensitivity and (3) a signal reporter moiety to respond to GLP by the selective recognition of u3-OH groups in Zr-O clusters toward GLP [18]. The open pores of PCN-222 facilitated mass diffusion of GLP to interact with recognition sites, thus achieving a fast response of 3 min. Specifically, the GLP triggered intrinsic color transformation of the sensor under nature light, immensely simplifying the design of the portable device by utilizing a built-in flashlight in a smartphone as a light source without introducing additional light sources, filters and power sources. In addition, a logic gate that utilized GLP concentration and G/R as the input and output signals was designed for intelligent monitoring. The developed smartphone-assisted portable platform integrated with N-CDs@PCN-222 offered a new strategy for intelligently semi-quantitative monitoring of GLP in practical application.

All agents were analytical reagent grade and used as obtained without further purification. Citric acid, tris(hydroxymethyl)methyl aminomethane (Tris), zirconium(IV) chloride (ZrCl4), benzoic acid, tetrakis(4-carboxyphenyl)porphyrin) (TCPP), Amberlite®IRA402, glyphosate (GLP) as well as others pesticides, for instance, thiophanate-methyl (TM), 2, 4-dichloro phenoxy acetic acid (2,4-D), carbendazim (MBC), imidacloprid (IMI), temephos, trichlorfon, dimethoate, phosmet, paraoxon, dichlorvos (DDVP), carbaryl and chlorbenzuron (Chl) were provided by Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) and Primary-secondary amine (PSA) packing were respectively supplied by Chengdu Kelon Chemical Reagent Factory (Chengdu, Sichuan, China) and ANPEL Laboratory Technologies (Shanghai) Inc (Shanghai, China). Polyethylene glycol (PEG 6000), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), magnesium chloride hexahydrate (MgCl2·6H2O), calcium chloride (CaCl2), zinc chloride (ZnCl2) and iron trichloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O) were provided by Xilong Chemical Industry Co. ltd. (Shantou, Guangdong, China). Saccharides, such as sucrose (Suc) and glucose (Glu) and amino acids such as phenylalanine (Phe), cysteine (Cys), glycine (Gly), and tryptophan (Try) were obtained from Guangdong Chemical Regent Engineering-technological Research and Development Center (Shantou, Guangdong, China). Deionized water was used throughout the experimental procedures. Polyethersulfone (PES) membrane was supported by Jiangsu Green Union Science Instrument Co., ltd. (Taizhou, Jiangsu, China). The food samples were purchased from a local Hao & Duo supermarket (Yangling, Shaanxi, PR China). Tap water (pH = 7.56) and two kinds of river water samples were respectively sampled from a laboratory faucet, lacquer river (pH = 6.93) and Wei river (pH = 7.94).

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and elemental mapping characterizations were conducted by a Hitachi S-4800f (Tokyo, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was further performed to survey morphology and microstructure of different materials on TECNAI G2 SPIRIT BIO (FEI, Hillsboro, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were carried out using a Vetex70 FT-IR Spectroscope (Bruker Corp, Karlsruhe, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were collected on an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Axis Ultra DLD, Kratos, UK). The phase structures of different materials were collected by Bruker D8 Advance A25 (Bruker Corp, Karlsruhe, Germany). A TGA 5500 (TA company, New Castle DE, USA) was used to thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) in N2 atmosphere. An MIR ASAP2460 Automatic specific surface and porosity analyzer (Micromeritics Instruments Co., Norcross, Georgia, USA) was used to investigate surface area and porous properties. All fluorescence data were collected on a fluorescence spectrometer F98 (Shanghai Lingguang Technology Co., ltd, Shanghai, China) with an excitation and emission slit widths of 10 nm. The UV–vis spectra were collected by a UV-2550 Spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Tokyo, Japan). In addition, an F7100 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) was utilized to investigate fluorescence lifetimes of different FL materials.

The N-CDs was prepared in accordance with a previous report with certain modifications [35]. Detailedly, 1 mmol citric acid and 1 mmol Tris were dissolved in 10 mL deionized water under ultrasonication to obtain a transparent solution, which was transferred into a 50 mL poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (Xi'an Yibell Instrument Equipment Co., ltd., Xian, China) and hydrothermally heated at 200 °C for 6 h. The reaction kettle was naturally cooled down at room temperature after the reaction. After filtering with 0.22 μm PES membrane, the yellow N-CDs solution was obtained and stored as a stock solution. The concentration of N-CDs was determined to be 0.75 mg/mL by lyophilizing a certain volume N-CDs solution. The N-CDs were diluted 100 times (7.5 μg/mL) with deionized water for further FL experiments.
PCN-222 was synthesized according to the literature [36] with some modifications. The detailed information was shown in Support Information (SI).

N-CDs@PCN-222 was synthesized by in situ encapsulating N-CDs into PCN-222. In detail, ZrCl4 (0.12 g), TCPP (0.04 g), benzoic acid (1.20 g), PEG 6000 (0.40 g) and CTAB (0.40 g) were mixed and ultrasonically dissolved (10 min) in 8 mL of DMF. The N-CDs was dropwise added into the mixture under violently magnetic stirring and further stirred 2 h at room temperature to completely react. After that, the mixture solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated in an oven at 120 °C for 12 h. After the specimen was naturally cooled down to room temperature, the product was centrifuged at 8,000 rpm for 10 min to remove the supernatant. Thereafter, the obtained purple precipitate of N-CDs@PCN-222 was separately washed with DMF, ethanol and H2O several times. Finally, the products were dried under 60 ◦C overnight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RF sensing of GLP on a spectrophotometer
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing parameters were systematically optimized to improve the sensing performance of the sensor for GLP. Typically, 40 μL N-CDs@PCN-222 was mixed with gradient GLP standard solution, and the volume of the sensing system was maintained at 1.5 mL with distilled water. After incubating for 3 min under nature temperature, the FL spectra were obtained at a 360 nm excitation wavelength. The selectivity was tested by examining the RF response of interference substances such as ions, amino acids and saccharides with respective 100-fold, 10-fold and 5-fold concentration of GLP. Further, adsorption experiments for GLP with N-CDs@PCN-222 were conducted to investigate the pre-concentration effect of PCN-222, and residual GLP concentrations were determined on a UV-2550 spectrophotometer [37]. The adsorption capacity Qt (mg g−1) and solubility partition coefficient of GLP for the N-CDs@PCN-222/water system were determined via empirical equations [13].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual quantification of GLP with smartphone
ce:section.ce:para: To avoid potentially environmental interference for realizing the in-field analysis of GLP, a portable sensing device was tactfully fabricated by utilizing a smartphone as a signal reader instead of conventional spectrometers. The smartphone-integrated platform was composed of a smartphone (Huawei nova5 iPro, Huawei Technologies Co., ltd, Shenzhen, China) and 3D-printed accessory including smartphone holder (83 × 70 × 75 mm), sample slot (10 × 10 × 10 mm) and a photoing cavity. The layout model of the portable mini-device was designed by Rhino3D and was then loaded into Cura software for slicing. After that, the file was sent to a 3D printer (Senlang i3) to start print using white resin as printing material.

In a typical visual assay, various concentrations of GLP were mixed with the sensor in colorimetric plates followed by placing them into the sample slot of the platform. The colorimetric photos were captured by the smartphone under the illumination of a built-in flashlight and were readily processed by the Color Recognizer Application (Xiyi Technology Co., ltd., Xiamen, China) installed in the smartphone to obtain the RGB color parameters.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GLP in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practicability of the sensor was further estimated by detecting GLP in spiked water samples and cereal samples (rice, millet). These samples were pretreated by a modified QuEChERS method [38]. In detail, the rice and millet samples were smashed and filtered using a 200 mesh sieve. 2 g food samples were mixed with 10 mL H2O under vortex for 3 min. Thereafter, the samples were ultrasonicated (10 min) and centrifuged (5000 rpm, 8 min) to collect supernatants. The water sample was centrifuged (5000 rpm, 8 min) and filtered with 0.22 μm PES membrane to remove large particles. 0.2 g PSA packing was added into these supernatants and the water sample for vortexing 3 min to remove organic acids, pigments, lipid compounds and metal ions. These supernatants were centrifuged (5000 rpm, 8 min) and mixed with 0.2 g IRA-400-OH resin to remove inorganic anions [39]. The supernatants were collected by centrifuging and filtered by 0.22 µm PES microporous membranes. Finally, each sample was spiked with GLP standard solutions to obtain final concentrations of 0.5, 2, 5 mg/L and was detected by the RF channel on a FL spectrophotometer (F98) and the visual method by the smartphone-integrated platform.

The practical feasibility was evaluated by HPLC-MS/MS on AB SCIEX 4500 equipped with Waters C18 chromatographic column at injection volume 10 µL and flow rate of 0.4 mL/min. Acetonitrile and 0.1 % formic acid water were used in the mobile phase. The ion source used electrospray ionization (ESI+) and has a temperature at 550 °C.

All fluorescence, colorimetric (RGB values) and UV–vis absorbance data (n = 3) were collected and statistically analyzed using Minitab 17.0 software (Minitab Inc., USA). To evaluate whether there was a significant difference between groups, one-way ANOVA and Tukey-HSD were used. Different significance tests between the proposed method and a referenced method were subjected to a student's test.

The N-CDs@PCN-222 was prepared by in situ encapsulating N-CDs into the PCN-222 through a one-pot solvothermal route. The dosage of N-CDs was optimized to improve RF sensing performance and achieve an obvious visual color change under sunlight, and the corresponding product was denoted as N-CDs@PCN-222(X) (X = 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, and 1 mL). As shown in Fig. S1 , noticeable Fr (the FL intensity ratio at 663 and 458 nm) and color change from pink purple to light green were observed after adding GLP to N-CDs@PCN-222(0.2), clarifying the optimal dosage of 0.2 mL N-CDs for synthesizing N-CDs@PCN-222 (P < 0.05).

From SEM images (Fig. 1
A and B), the PCN-222 displayed an archetypical cubic close-packed morphology with an approximately regular diameter of 450 nm ( Fig. S2
A). N-CDs@PCN-222 presented an irregular cubic structure with blurred edges and corners (Fig. 1 D and E) and an average size of 80.51 nm ( Fig. S2
B). Clearly, N-CDs had a slight effect on the PCN-222 morphology, which may be due to that N-CDs restrained the spatial growth of PCN-222 and the combination of N-CDs and Zr4+ also hindered the formation of a regular cubic morphology [18]. The TEM results (Fig. 1 C and F) further verified the size and shape of PCN-222 and N-CDs@PCN-222. Besides, TEM and HRTEM images (Fig. 1 G) demonstrated that the N-CDs were embedded in the N-CDs@PCN-222 with about 0.22 nm of lattice spacing (inset), which can be ascribed to the (100) diffraction plane of graphitic carbon, implying the presence of N-CDs. The results were similar to previous studies [40,41]. The decreased size change after the introduction of N-CDs can also be verified by the corresponding colloidal stability of PCN-222 and N-CDs@PCN-222. As shown in Fig. S3 , both PCN-222 and N-CDs@PCN-222 possessed no obvious difference at initial. But after standing for 24 h, apparent delamination was observed for PCN-222 while not for N-CDs@PCN-222, which may be due to the decreased size enabling N-CDs@PCN-222 to be more stable. Element mapping images ( Fig. S4 ) clearly revealed the presence of Zr, C, O and N in N-CDs@PCN-222. The TGA curves (Fig. 1 H) illustrated that the initial weight loss at 35–100 °C was accounted for the removal of H2O due to the dehydration of Zr6O4(OH)4 clusters. Subsequently, within the 275–430 °C temperature range, the decomposition of TCPP ligand enclosed within PCN-222 pores induced a dramatic weight loss [42]. The significant weight loss over 550 °C was caused by the framework collapse, and N-CDs@PCN-222 possessed higher thermal stability than pristine PCN-222. Additionally, the N-CDs@PCN-222 exhibited a prototypically reversible type-IV N2 adsorption/desorption isotherm (Fig. 1 I). The surface area of N-CDs@PCN-222 was ∼ 225.66 m2 g−1 with a total pore volume of 0.1114 cm3 g−1 and a mesoporous size distribution of 5.6428 nm. The N-CDs@PCN-222 with large mesoporous and surface area was apparently more favorable to mass transfer of GLP.

The XRD pattern of PCN-222 (Fig. 2A) was consistent with the simulated one [36], indicating the triumphant synthesis of PCN-222. It should be noted that the broader diffraction peaks of the PCN-222 than the simulated ones may be due to its nanostructure and the phenomenon was similar to previous works [36,43]. Further, its crystalline structure of PCN-222 was well inherited in N-CDs@PCN-222, validating that the encapsulation process did not change the crystalline structure of PCN-222. From FT-IR spectra in Fig. 2B, a broad adsorption of PCN-222 centered at ∼ 3440 cm−1 and the peak ∼ 2850 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibration of C-OH and –CH2. The CO (1637 cm−1) and CO vibration peaks (1467 and 1404 cm−1) were proof of terminal coordination between carboxylate groups in the TCPP ligand and Zr4+ [44,45]. The absorption peaks at 1562 and 885 cm−1 were accounted for by the NH bending vibration of porphyrin ring and the skeleton vibrations of the benzene rings of TCPP [46]. After encapsulating N-CDs, the hypochromatic shifts of adsorption peaks from 1637, 1467 and 1404 cm−1 to 1631, 1460 and 1382 cm−1 validated that the synthesis of N-CDs@PCN-222 changed the carboxylate groups by coordinating N-CDs groups with Zr4+. The XPS spectra (Fig. 2
C) illustrated that both PCN-222 and N-CDs@PCN-222 were composed of C1s, N1s, O1s and Zr 3d. The deconvoluted O1s spectra of PCN-222 (Fig. 2 D) displayed three peaks at binding energies of 530.53, 531.65, and 532.46 eV, which were respectively ascribed to Zr-O-Zr, Zr-OH2 and OC-O. The high-resolution spectrum of Zr3d (Fig. 2 E) can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 182.85 and 185.23 eV, which were associated with Zr3d5/2 and Zr3d3/2 of Zr-OH. After hybriding with N-CDs, both O1s and Zr3d were slightly shifted to lower energies in N-CDs@PCN-222 compared with PCN-222. This could because of the electron-donation effect of N-CDs from –COOH to Zr4+, which resulted in the increase of atoms electron density [47]. These characterizations suggested the successful preparation of N-CDs@PCN-222 by encapsulating N-CDs into the PCN-222 framework.

It can be seen from Fig. 3
A that the UV–vis spectrum of PCN-222 was coincident with that of N-CDs@PCN-222. The maximum absorption peak at 433 nm was a typical Soret bond of porphyrin derived from S0 → S2 transition of TCPP. Four shoulder peaks ranging from 500 to 700 nm were relatively weak Q bands arising from S0 → S1 transition of TCPP. Subsequently, the fluorescence characteristics of N-CDs, TCPP ligand, PCN-222 and N-CDs@PCN-222 were systematically investigated. The fluorescent spectrum of N-CDs (Fig. 3 B) presented an emission peak around 410 nm under excitation of 340 nm and displayed a bright blue fluorescence under a 365 nm UV light (the inset). Fig. 3 C also showed that the N-CDs possessed a well-distinguished emission centered at 410 nm. The excitation-independent property may be caused by a uniform surface state, which is conducive to FL assay by reducing autofluorescence interference during detection [48]. As shown in Fig. 3 D, TCPP ligand possessed strong fluorescence at 663 nm, while PCN-222 hardly exhibited emission. This may be because the Zr4+ with superparamagnetism can result in heavy atom effects and thus introduce some degree of relaxation into the spin-forbidden singlet–triplet transitions [49]. Thereby, the FL emission of TCPP at 663 nm was quenched when PCN-222 was synthesized. Fortunately, the absorption band of PCN-222 largely overlapped with the emission band of N-CDs. Thus, it was anticipated that a Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) process would occur when integrating N-CDs with PCN-222, which can be utilized to construct a dual-emitting platform [11]. As expected, N-CDs@PCN-222 exhibited red emission under irritation of a 365 nm UV lamp (Inset of Fig. 3 D) and possessed dual emissions at 458 nm and 663 nm, which were respectively originated from N-CDs and TCPP ligand in PCN-222, and the optimal excitation wavelength was 360 nm (Fig. 3 E). While N-CDs + PCN-222 system by simple physically mixing displayed a single emission (Fig. 3 D). This is because FRET would occur and also rely on an effective distance between donor and acceptor besides spectral overlapping [50]. The fluorophore acceptor (TCPP in PCN-222) was confined in cages of PCN-222, when simply mixing N-CDs with PCN-222, may no efficient energy transfer occur due to the large Förster distance. However, when the N-CDs were confined to the MOF pores by the encapsulation method, energy transfer can be generated due to the shorter distance. After encapsulating N-CDs into PCN-222, the emission of N-CDs displayed a bathochromic shift of 48 nm (410 nm → 458 nm), implying the interplay between N-CDs and PCN-222, which was in accordance with the results of FTIR and XPS. Finally, after 60 min of irritation with a 365 nm UV light, the Fr of N-CDs@PCN-222 was practically constant (P > 0.05), indicating excellent photobleaching resistance (Fig. 3 F).

The adsorption performance of N-CDs@PCN-222 for GLP was explored to evaluate the pre-concentration effect. As illustrated in Fig. S5
A, the adsorption capacity Qt (mg g−1) of GLP increased conspicuously in the initial 25 min until reaching saturation after 45 min (P > 0.05) with an equilibrium adsorption capacity (Qe ) of 188.2 mg g−1. To further reveal adsorption behavior, three kinetic models including pseudo-first-order model (Eq.1), pseudo-second kinetics expressed as (Eq.
(2) ) and intra-particle diffusion model (Eq.
(3) ) were utilized to fit the experimental adsorption data.

The corresponding fitted parameters for the adsorption toward GLP were tabulated in Table S1 . Evidently, in comparison to pseudo-first-order, pseudo-second-order acquired a higher coefficient of determination (R2) and more matched computed q t, suggesting chemisorption between GLP and the binding sites of N-CDs@PCN-222. For further interpretation of diffusion mechanism during adsorption process, the fitting linear plot between Qt and t0.5 derived from intra-particle diffusion model was demonstrated in Fig. S5
B. Notably, multi-linearity correlations illuminated that the GLP adsorption process was involved in three stages. In the first stage, instantaneous mass transfer of GLP from aqueous solution to N-CDs@PCN-222 surface by boundary layer diffusion produced a high slope. Afterward, due to the considerably lower molecular size of GLP (7.14 × 2.68 × 3.02 Å3) [51] than the pore size of N-CDs@PCN-222, the transferred GLP molecules underwent intra-particle diffusion migrating from the surface of N-CDs@PCN-222 to pores or cavities. Finally, the adsorption equilibrium was obtained in the last step.

Moreover, the large solubility partition coefficients of ∼ 1989.09 for GLP in N-CDs@PCN-222/water system implied that GLP was preferentially inclined to access the pores of N-CDs@PCN-222, verifying the pre-concentration effect. The splendid adsorption performance of N-CDs@PCN-222 was critical in allowing it to interact with GLP more sufficient for more sensitive detection.

To improve the sensing sensitivity for RF detection, the experimental parameters were systematically optimized. From Fig. S6
A and B, the maximum ΔFr (the changed value of Fr in the absence and presence of GLP) of the sensor was observed in H2O (P < 0.05) and at 47.2 µg/mL of the sensor (P < 0.05), hence the following experiments were conducted in deionized water and 47.2 µg/mL N-CDs@PCN-222. As displayed in Fig. S6
C, Fr was markedly decreased in the first 30 s and reached equilibrium at 3 min (P > 0.05). The exceptionally short equilibrium time revealed a quick response between N-CDs@PCN-222 and GLP, which benefited from the pre-concentration effect triggered by the porous structure of PCN-222. As seen from Fig. S6
D, the incubation temperature had an insignificant effect on GLP sensing (P > 0.05), implying striking temperature adaptation of the sensor. Herein, room temperature was considered as the optimum reaction temperature.

Under optimal parameters, different concentrations of GLP were mixed with N-CDs@PCN-222 (47.2 µg/mL) in distilled water and incubated for 3 min at room temperature. The corresponding FL spectra in Fig. 4
A demonstrated that both the FL of N-CDs and PCN-222 of the sensor exhibited a synchronous quenching response by GLP. As exhibited in Fig. 4 B, Fr vs GLP concentrations possessed a commendable linear relationship (R2 = 0.9946) in the range of 0.01–6.67 mg/L, which can be described as a regression equation of Fr = -0.0298[C] + 0.3364 ([C]: GLP concentration, mg/L) with LOD of 9.06 µg/L (3σ/k). The comparison of various analytical platforms toward GLP ( Table S2 ) clarified that the RF sensor exhibited a wider linear range and lower limit detection without the requirement of enzymes. More crucially, an obvious color change (pink purple → green) under sunlight was observed by naked eyes with the increase of GLP concentrations (Fig. 4 C), which indicated that the visual detection of GLP by naked-eye or sensing terminal under nature light was feasible.

Furthermore, the selectivity was evaluated by testing the response of the sensor toward potential interferents. Fig. 4 D and 4E showed that Fr was remarkably decreased with the addition of GLP, and most of the tested interferents displayed negligible interference (P > 0.05) except Fe3+ and imidacloprid (IMI). Fortunately, Fe3+ can be removed by primary secondary amine (PSA) packing, and IMI would not be extracted with an aqueous solution during pretreatment. In summary, the nanosensor with good selectivity toward GLP can be applied to detect GLP in practical samples after proper pretreatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection and logic gate circuit
ce:section.ce:para: Integrating sensing platforms with molecule logic gates is advantageous for intelligently automated monitoring of analytes [14]. In this scenario, the normalized G/R obtained by the platform (output signal: Coutput) and GLP concentration (input: Cinput) were utilized to construct a one-to-one logic circuit. The presence and absence of Cinput were defined as codes of 1 and 0 by the Boolean logic function, and the threshold was set to 0.79. From Fig. 5C, the defined color channel ratio was 1 with respect to G/R > 0.79, while the color ratio was 0 for G/R ≤ 0.79. The relevant truth table suggested that G/R can decode GLP amounts variation via logical categories of [NOT (0, 0) and YES (1, 1)] gates. The Cinput of this logic circuit contained eight different concentrations of GLP (0.20–6.67 mg/L) by utilizing the normalized G/R as the output signal (Fig. 5D). As shown in Fig. 5E, a YES gate was triggered when Cinput was 0.67 mg/L, while a NOT gate for Cinput ≤ 0.67 mg/L. The standard colorimetric cards corresponding to different GLP concentrations were also demonstrated in Fig. 5 F. The logic analytical device, which was incorporated with the smartphone-assisted platform, can actualize intelligently semi-quantitative monitoring of GLP via colorimetric and exhibit favorable sensing performance.

Considering that PCN-222 hardly exhibited fluorescence emission, we studied the response of individual N-CDs and TCPP ligand to GLP. As presented in Fig. 6
A, TCPP presented significantly FL quenching after incubated with GLP ( Fig. S9 ) while FL intensity of individual N-CDs exhibited no significant change ( Fig. S10 ), suggesting that TCPP ligand of PCN-222 in N-CDs@PCN-222 was response unit of GLP detection signal. However, upon addition of GLP into the N-CDs@PCN-222, both the FL of N-CDs (F458) and PCN-222 (F663) in N-CDs@PCN-222 were quenched (refer to Fig. 4 A). The FL quenching mechanism of PCN-222 was firstly studied. Literatures demonstrated that FL of luminescent MOF (LMOF) can be quenched mainly resulted from framework collapse, electron and/or energy transfer between LMOF and analytes [52]. As shown in Fig. 2 A, diffraction peaks of N-CDs@PCN-222 before and after incubated with GLP exhibited were consistent, implying that the interaction between GLP and N-CDs@PCN-222 had no effect on crystalline structure of N-CDs@PCN-222. Whereas, after GLP interacting with N-CDs@PCN-222, the relative intensities of characteristic peaks became weaker than N-CDs@PCN-222. This phenomenon may be attributed to that GLP was trapped in the N-CDs@PCN-222 cavities, resulting in the diminished X-ray contrast between porous framework and pore cages [53]. Besides, a fluorescence enhancement should be occurred due to the release of TCPP ligand when framework was destructed by target [49]. However, in case of the study, the fluorescence of N-CDs@PCN-222 regarding PCN-222 moiety was quenched upon addition of GLP. On the other hand, as shown in Fig. S11 , no obvious framework collapse was observed from SEM and TEM images of N-CDs@PCN-222 + GLP. Overall, we can accordingly conclude that GLP cannot cause the framework collapse. Fig. 6 B showed that there was no obvious spectral overlapping between UV–vis absorption of GLP and FL excitation/emission spectra of N-CDs@PCN-222, ruling out the presence of energy transfer. The UV–vis spectra of N-CDs@PCN-222 before and after adding GLP (Fig. 6 C) showed obvious difference, indicating that a complex was formed between N-CDs@PCN-222 and GLP. Previous literature has reported that PCN-222 can be regarded as a semiconductor [20] due to electron-rich TCPP, and GLP was electron-deficient which can act as an efficient electron acceptor [5]. A photo-induced electron transfer (PET) process was therefore speculated as a possible quenching mechanism. Additionally, the FL lifetime of N-CDs@PCN-222 (Fig. 6 D) decreased from 1.11 ns to 0.69 ns after adding GLP, which was accordant with PET feature. In a word, the quenching mechanism of PCN-222 moiety caused by GLP can be ascribed to a PET principle, in which GLP can coordinate with groups on the surface of N-CDs@PCN-222 to form a complex that would initiate non-radiative emissions when the excited electrons return to the ground state and thus quench FL of PCN-222. Subsequently, the quenching mechanism of N-CDs was also studied. Fig. S12 indicated that the UV–vis absorption peak of PCN-222 + GLP overlapped with the FL excitation/emission spectrum of N-CDs, thus inner filter effect (IFE) could occur from N-CDs to PCN-222 + GLP complex, causing FL quenching of N-CDs. The unsignificant change of FL lifetime of N-CDs from 12.21 ns to 12.20 ns ( Fig. S13 ) further verified the IFE phenomenon [50].

The pink purple of N-CDs@PCN-222 originated from the highly conjugated π-electron system of the TCPP porphyrin ligand, which featured with intense Soret band in the near-UV region and the relatively weak Q band in the visible region (refer to Fig. 3 A) [54]. Besides, the porphyrin electronic system can be affected by peripheral conjugative disturbance, allowing rational electronic fine-tuning to impetuously change its optical and electronic properties. Several literatures indicated that the basic N-atoms of TCPP with four fold-symmetry and planar conformation can accept acidic protons to generate strong configuration interactions [54,55]. Thus, we hypothesized that the acid herbicide of GLP containing phosphoric acid and carboxylic acid groups could provide protons, which lead to protonation of the N-atoms of the TCPP within PCN-222. Due to Q bands in 500–550 nm corresponding to the neutral porphyrin MOFs was highly vulnerable to protonation, therefore the transformation of TCPP from a neutral plane to protonated form can be verified by UV–vis absorbance [56]. As a proof-of-concept, the UV–vis spectra of the sensor in the presence of different concentrations of GLP were investigated (Fig. 7
A). As expected, the peaks in Q-band around 500–550 nm decreased and even disappeared toward the increase of GLP concentration. From FTIR spectra of N-CDs@PCN-222 (refer to Fig. 2 B), the two peaks at 1562 and 1382 cm−1 associated with the NH bending of porphyrin ring and CO bond of carboxylate groups disappeared after interacting with GLP, which may be caused by H-bonding interaction between GLP and the N-CDs@PCN-222. This further proved the protonation of TCPP inside the PCN-222 [55]. Enlightened by the above facts, we proposed that the protonation of the pyrrolic ring nitrogens caused the porphyrin of PCN-222 to change from neutral planar to a protonated form, which could account for the unique color variation triggered by GLP.

The three main XPS peaks of N1s (Fig. 7 B) at 397.69, 399.86 and 402.05 eV were respectively ascribed to CN, CN and NH, which shifted to 397.76, 399.94 and 402.19 eV after GLP capture and were accompanied by the increased NH proportion. This indicated that the N electron density decreased may be due to hydrogen bonding interactions between GLP and N-CDs@PCN-222. The O1s spectra (Fig. 7 C) were deconvolved into three peaks at ∼ 530.44 (Zr-O-Zr), 531.47 (Zr-OH2) and 532.17 eV (OC-O), which were shifted to 530.72, 531.67 and 532.44 eV after GLP capture. Moreover, a new peak at 531.35 eV validated the forming of ZrOP/PO, revealing that Zr-OH of N-CDs@PCN-222 can be served as anchored sites to identify GLP via phosphate groups. The fraction of OC-O decreased from 50.16 % to 25.05 %, implying that oxygen-containing moieties was involved in GLP capture. The binding energies of Zr3d (Fig. 7 D) derived from Zr3d5/2 and Zr3d3/2 of Zr-OH increased from 182.81 and 185.19 eV to 183.22 and 185.67 eV. This may be due to a mutual effect between zirconium clusters and strongly electron-withdrawing GLP, which caused the decrease of the electron density of Zr [52]. Simultaneously, ZrOP species were formed after GLP capture which can be evidenced by the peaks of 182.63 and 185.11 eV. On the other hand, the decreased proportion of Zr-OH along with the large fractions of Zr-P species further revealed chemical adsorption of GLP. These results implied that N-CDs@PCN-222 captured GLP mainly through chemical interactions between phosphate groups of GLP molecules and Zr-OH or oxygen-containing moieties of the sensor as well as hydrogen-bonding interaction. As mentioned above, multiple physical and chemical interplays participated in the sensing process and illustrated the specificity toward GLP in complicated matrices. Delightfully, the N-CDs@PCN-222 with mesoporous, large surface area and abundant binding motifs facilitated mass diffusion of GLP to N-CDs@PCN-222 by reducing the transfer resistance, thus enhancing pre-concentration toward GLP. The strong pre-enrichment further conferred the N-CDs@PCN-222 nanosensor with desirable analytical performance to sensitive detecting GLP.

GLP residue in real samples was determined by the sensor in terms of RF mode and the portable platform. No GLP was found in the real samples ( Fig. S14 ) by HPLC-MS/MS, and spiked recoveries experiments were subsequently conducted. As tabulated in Table S4 , the desirable recovery rates of 94.0–112.5 % (RSD < 5.21 %) and 90.0–113.6 % (RSD < 7.11 %) were respectively achieved by RF detection and the smartphone-integrated platform visual readout. Furthermore, the practical reliability of the sensor was evaluated by comprising with HPLC-MS/MS. The results detected by HPLC-MS/MS ( Table S5 ) were consistent with RF (P > 0.05) and platform detection (P > 0.05), suggesting that the RF and colorimetric platform can quantify GLP in real samples with good accuracy and reliability.

Herein, a novel dual-modal sensing strategy regarding sensitive and reliable detection of GLP was proposed using the N-CDs@PCN-222 nanosensor as a fluorescence and colorimetric generator. The N-CDs@PCN-222, which was facilely synthesized by in situ encapsulating N-CDs into PCN-222, can detect GLP by dual-mode of an RF channel on a spectrophotometer and a colorimetric on a portable smartphone platform. The large surface area and mesoporous nature of PCN-222 resulted in a substantial pre-concentration effect, allowing GLP to interact with recognition sites more rapidly and sufficiently, as well as with high sensitivity. More interestingly, a one-to-one colorimetric logic gate circuit was further developed as an intelligent sensing platform, allowing the nanosensor to be potentially implemented in the field of smart devices. In conclusion, the study shed light on developing a promising dual-mode platform for sensitively, reliably and visually quick pre-warning GLP.
